content: It’s reasonable to be skeptical about blockchain, especially when it’s often described as a perfect solution. At its core, blockchain is simply a digital ledger—a record of transactions—shared across many computers instead of kept by one central authority like a bank. Normally, a bank verifies and records every transaction, acting as a trusted middleman. Blockchain replaces that by having multiple participants, or “nodes,” each keep a copy of the entire transaction history. When a new transaction occurs, these nodes group it with others into a “block” and follow agreed-upon rules to verify it. Once confirmed, the block is added to a chain of previous blocks, creating a secure and chronological record. Because everyone holds a copy, if someone tries to change a past transaction, the others will notice and reject it. This system doesn’t rely on any single institution, reducing the chance of fraud or error. While not perfect, blockchain offers a transparent way to record transactions without a central bank.
